refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output (energy_per_ton) to support evaluation against a customer-defined energy efficiency target.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the capability to measure/report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a performance target. The refined FR focuses on the functional requirement—enabling visibility and traceability of energy efficiency data—which is solution-neutral and within system control. Achievement of the target (E_target ≤ energy_per_ton) is a performance acceptance criterion, not a functional requirement. This separation clarifies that the system must provide the measurement infrastructure; actual target achievement depends on design choices (DP/IM) and operational conditions.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that the NGHE solution collects, calculates, and reports energy_per_ton metrics with defined accuracy and frequency over the agreed evaluation period, and that reported values can be independently validated against energy input and work output measurements.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall measure total energy consumed (kWh or equivalent) during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall measure or infer total work output (tons moved or equivalent unit) during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall calculate energy_per_ton as the ratio of energy consumed to work output.
FR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall record and report energy_per_ton metrics with defined temporal granularity and accuracy to authorized stakeholders.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument energy input (fuel/electricity) at source; instrument work output (tonnage moved) at point of delivery; compute ratio offline post-evaluation period; report against target.
DP2: Embed real-time energy and tonnage sensors; stream data to onboard compute module; calculate energy_per_ton continuously; trigger alerts if threshold breached; log for post-evaluation reporting.

DP3: Integrate energy metering at power distribution node; integrate tonnage measurement via load cells or displacement sensors; synchronize timestamps; aggregate over evaluation window; compute and report energy_per_ton with confidence bounds.

DP4: Establish energy consumption baseline via controlled calibration runs; measure incremental energy and tonnage during operational use; normalize for environmental/operational variance; report delta energy_per_ton against target.

DP5: Decompose energy consumption into sub-system contributions (propulsion, hydraulics, auxiliary); measure tonnage at each work cycle; allocate energy to work output per subsystem; aggregate to total energy_per_ton; enable root-cause analysis.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP3: Integrate energy metering at power distribution node; integrate tonnage measurement via load cells or displacement sensors; synchronize timestamps; aggregate over evaluation window; compute and report energy_per_ton with confidence bounds.

potential_sources: Energy metering and tonnage measurement are logically independent (different physical phenomena, different sensor modalities); decoupling via timestamp synchronization avoids tight temporal coupling.
Aggregation and reporting logic is decoupled from sensor acquisition; allows independent evolution of measurement hardware and data processing.
Confidence bounds introduce statistical rigor without coupling to specific sensor types or calibration methods.
Minor coupling risk: synchronization protocol must be defined; recommend loose coupling via event timestamps rather than hard real-time synchronization.
coupling_check.notes: DP3 avoids the tight coupling of DP2 (continuous real-time compute) and the post-hoc analysis burden of DP1. DP4 introduces baseline variance modeling (higher complexity, potential coupling to environmental sensors). DP5 introduces subsystem decomposition (valuable for diagnostics but increases measurement complexity and coupling to subsystem boundaries). DP3 balances simplicity, independence, and traceability.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Energy metering can be installed at a single power distribution node without loss of fidelity (i.e., auxiliary loads and parasitic losses are either negligible or consistently attributable to work output). Risk: If auxiliary systems (HVAC, lighting, control electronics) consume significant energy independent of tonnage, energy_per_ton will be inflated. Mitigation: Conduct baseline energy audit; consider subsystem metering if auxiliary load is >10% of total.

Assumption: Tonnage measurement is accurate and representative of work output. Risk: Load cells may drift; displacement-based estimates may not account for partial loads or inefficient paths. Mitigation: Calibrate sensors pre-evaluation; cross-check tonnage via independent method (e.g., weighbridge spot checks).

Assumption: Evaluation period is long enough to average transient effects (startup, shutdown, idle). Risk: Short evaluation windows may yield high variance in energy_per_ton. Mitigation: Define minimum evaluation period (e.g., ≥100 work cycles or ≥7 days operational time); report confidence intervals.

Assumption: Timestamp synchronization is achievable with acceptable latency (<<1 second). Risk: If energy and tonnage measurements are not synchronized, ratio computation may be biased. Mitigation: Use common time source (GPS, NTP, or local clock with drift compensation); validate synchronization error <100 ms.

Assumption: Customer-defined E_target is realistic and achievable given equipment design. Risk: If target is unachievable, measurement system will correctly report failure but cannot improve outcome. Mitigation: Validate target against baseline performance data before evaluation period begins.

Assumption: Worksite safety plan does not prohibit sensor installation or data logging. Risk: Safety constraints may limit sensor placement or require additional guarding. Mitigation: Coordinate with safety officer early; design sensor enclosures and wiring to meet site requirements.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output (energy_per_ton = total_energy_used / total_tonnage_moved) over an agreed evaluation period, with synchronized energy and tonnage data streams, aggregated with documented confidence bounds, to support objective evaluation against a customer-defined energy efficiency target E_target. The measurement system shall be independent of propulsion technology and shall account for (or explicitly exclude) auxiliary energy consumption per the customer's definition of 'work output.' Measurement accuracy and evaluation period shall be jointly defined with the customer before evaluation begins.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized energy metering, tonnage measurement integration, timestamp synchronization, aggregation logic, and energy-per-ton computation with confidence bounds reporting.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmit computed energy_per_ton metrics, confidence bounds, and aggregated measurements to external monitoring/analytics systems.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receive evaluation window parameters, metering configuration, and measurement sensor specifications from task orchestration.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communicate with distributed power distribution nodes and load cell/displacement sensor endpoints to collect raw energy and tonnage data.

module_containment_scores.notes: Highest cohesion: energy metering, tonnage measurement, synchronization, aggregation, and reporting are all core NGHE operational analytics. Minimal diffusion: only external interfaces are data ingestion (Network Link) and output (Telemetry). Timestamp synchronization and confidence bound computation are naturally housed in a centralized solution layer.

Poor fit: Vehicle is a physical/operational entity, not an analytics platform. Energy metering and tonnage aggregation logic would diffuse across multiple vehicle instances. Computation of energy_per_ton requires cross-vehicle aggregation, which cannot be localized to a single vehicle boundary.

Weak containment: Teleoperation is a control/command interface, not a data aggregation or analytics module. Energy metering and tonnage measurement are operational concerns, not teleoperation concerns. High diffusion: logic would leak into Vehicle and Safety Services.

Misaligned scope: Safety Services is for hazard detection and mitigation, not energy/tonnage analytics. While energy efficiency could inform safety thresholds, the primary DP (metering, measurement, aggregation, reporting) is orthogonal to safety concerns. Significant diffusion if forced here.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The DP is fundamentally a data integration, aggregation, and analytics function that operates at the system level across multiple vehicles and power distribution nodes. NGHE Solution is the natural owner because it is the centralized platform responsible for operational intelligence, efficiency metrics, and performance reporting. Energy-per-ton is a key efficiency KPI that belongs in the solution layer, not in vehicle-level or safety-level logic.
containment_risks: Risk: Sensor data latency and clock skew across distributed power nodes and vehicles may complicate timestamp synchronization. Mitigation: Implement NTP or GPS-based time synchronization protocol within NGHE Solution.

Risk: Load cell and displacement sensor heterogeneity (different manufacturers, calibration curves) may require sensor-specific adapters. Mitigation: Encapsulate sensor abstraction layer within NGHE Solution to normalize inputs before aggregation.

Risk: Confidence bound computation requires statistical model of measurement uncertainty. If model parameters vary by vehicle or sensor type, diffusion risk increases. Mitigation: Centralize uncertainty model in NGHE Solution; parameterize by sensor/vehicle metadata.

Risk: Real-time aggregation over evaluation windows may create tight coupling to Vehicle telemetry streams. Mitigation: Use asynchronous event-driven architecture (Telemetry interface) to decouple NGHE Solution from Vehicle timing.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP3 directly addresses the FR by specifying a complete measurement and reporting pipeline: energy metering at power distribution nodes, tonnage measurement via sensors, timestamp synchronization, aggregation over evaluation windows, and energy_per_ton computation with confidence bounds. The DP is logically sound and traces to the acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target). No logical gaps detected.

diffusion_risks: Timestamp synchronization logic: Risk of clock skew across distributed power nodes and vehicles. Mitigation embedded in DP but requires explicit NTP/GPS protocol implementation within NGHE Solution IM boundary.

Sensor abstraction layer: Load cell and displacement sensor heterogeneity (manufacturer-specific calibration curves) may leak into Vehicle or Network Link interfaces if not encapsulated. Recommend sensor adapter pattern within NGHE Solution to normalize inputs before aggregation.

Confidence bound computation: Statistical uncertainty model parameterization by sensor type and vehicle metadata creates coupling risk. If model parameters are scattered across Vehicle telemetry or Safety Services, diffusion score increases. Recommend centralizing uncertainty model in NGHE Solution with metadata-driven parameterization.

Real-time aggregation coupling: Tight synchronous coupling to Vehicle telemetry streams during evaluation windows may create latency bottlenecks. Recommend asynchronous event-driven architecture via Telemetry interface to decouple NGHE Solution from Vehicle timing constraints.

Evaluation window boundary conditions: Partial measurement windows at start/end of evaluation periods may require special handling (e.g., interpolation, exclusion logic). Risk of this logic diffusing into Vehicle or Tasking interfaces if not explicitly owned by NGHE Solution.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution IM boundary is well-scoped with containment_score 0.92. Energy metering, tonnage measurement integration, synchronization, aggregation, and reporting are cohesive analytics functions naturally housed in a centralized solution layer. External interfaces (Telemetry out, Tasking in, Network Link inout) are clean data boundaries. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services are correctly excluded (containment scores 0.35, 0.25, 0.3 respectively) because energy-per-ton is a system-level efficiency KPI, not a vehicle-local or safety-local concern. Ownership allocation to NGHE Solution is justified and minimizes diffusion.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) is directly measurable via NGHE Solution outputs. Verification requires: (1) calibration of energy metering and tonnage sensors against known standards, (2) validation of timestamp synchronization accuracy (e.g., ±100ms tolerance), (3) statistical validation of confidence bounds (e.g., coverage probability ≥95%), (4) end-to-end integration testing over representative evaluation windows. All verification activities are localized to NGHE Solution IM boundary and external sensor/power distribution interfaces. Feasibility is high.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR allocation to NGHE Solution IM is clear and unambiguous. The DP specifies five concrete responsibilities: (1) energy metering integration, (2) tonnage measurement integration, (3) timestamp synchronization, (4) aggregation logic, (5) energy_per_ton computation and reporting. All five are explicitly owned by NGHE Solution. No ambiguity regarding which module computes energy_per_ton or reports confidence bounds. Interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, Network Link) are well-defined with clear data flow directions. Allocation clarity is high.

recommended_revisions: Explicitly specify timestamp synchronization protocol (NTP, GPS, or PTP) and acceptable clock skew tolerance (e.g., ±100ms) in DP3 to reduce implementation ambiguity.

Add sensor abstraction layer design pattern to DP3: 'Encapsulate sensor-specific calibration curves and data normalization in a pluggable adapter interface within NGHE Solution to support heterogeneous load cell and displacement sensor types.'

Clarify confidence bound computation model in DP3: 'Implement statistical uncertainty model parameterized by sensor type, vehicle metadata, and measurement window duration. Centralize model in NGHE Solution; do not distribute to Vehicle or Safety Services.'

Specify evaluation window boundary handling in DP3: 'Define policy for partial measurement windows at start/end of evaluation periods (e.g., exclude, interpolate, or flag with reduced confidence). Implement policy logic within NGHE Solution aggregation module.'

Add asynchronous event-driven architecture guidance to DP3: 'Decouple NGHE Solution aggregation from Vehicle telemetry timing via event-driven Telemetry interface to avoid real-time latency coupling.'

Add acceptance test criteria to FR: 'Timestamp synchronization accuracy ≥95% within ±100ms; confidence bounds coverage probability ≥95%; sensor calibration drift <2% over evaluation period; energy_per_ton computation latency <5 minutes from end of evaluation window.'

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence scores (0.92 containment, high verification feasibility, high allocation clarity). However, the recommended_revisions list identifies five concrete design gaps that require explicit sub-level decomposition to resolve: (1) timestamp synchronization protocol specification, (2) sensor abstraction layer pattern, (3) confidence bound computation model, (4) evaluation window boundary handling, and (5) asynchronous event-driven architecture. These are not minor clarifications but represent distinct functional concerns that must be decomposed into sub-FRs to ensure implementation correctness and risk mitigation. Given high safety criticality, deferring these to implementation is unacceptable. Decomposition now ensures traceability and explicit ownership allocation at the next level.
next_level_sub_frs: FR3.1: Synchronize timestamps across distributed energy metering nodes and vehicle telemetry streams with clock skew tolerance ≤±100ms to ensure temporal coherence of energy and tonnage measurements within evaluation windows.

FR3.2: Normalize heterogeneous load cell and displacement sensor inputs (manufacturer-specific calibration curves, data formats, sampling rates) into a unified measurement abstraction to support plug-and-play sensor integration without diffusion into Vehicle or Network Link interfaces.

FR3.3: Compute statistical confidence bounds on energy_per_ton estimates by parameterizing uncertainty models with sensor type, vehicle metadata, and measurement window duration, with centralized model ownership to prevent diffusion into Vehicle or Safety Services.

FR3.4: Handle evaluation window boundary conditions (partial measurement windows at start/end of evaluation periods) via explicit policy (exclusion, interpolation, or confidence reduction) to prevent ambiguity and diffusion into Vehicle or Tasking interfaces.

FR3.5: Decouple NGHE Solution aggregation logic from Vehicle telemetry timing constraints via asynchronous event-driven architecture to avoid real-time latency bottlenecks and ensure aggregation latency ≤5 minutes from end of evaluation window.

revise.why: DP3 is logically sound but under-specified in five critical areas identified by diffusion risk analysis. Revise DP3 to explicitly incorporate: (1) NTP/GPS/PTP protocol selection with ±100ms tolerance specification, (2) sensor adapter pattern with pluggable calibration interface, (3) centralized statistical uncertainty model with metadata-driven parameterization, (4) evaluation window boundary policy with explicit handling rules, (5) event-driven Telemetry interface design to decouple aggregation timing. These revisions transform DP3 from a high-level concept into an implementable design that prevents diffusion and clarifies ownership boundaries. Revisions should be completed before sub-FR decomposition to ensure sub-DPs inherit clear architectural constraints.

assumptions: Timestamp synchronization tolerance of ±100ms is acceptable for energy-per-ton accuracy (assumes energy metering and tonnage measurement are not sub-second phenomena).
Sensor heterogeneity (load cell and displacement sensor types) can be abstracted via a pluggable adapter pattern without loss of fidelity or performance.
Statistical confidence bound model can be parameterized by sensor type and vehicle metadata without requiring real-time vehicle state feedback during aggregation.
Evaluation window boundary conditions occur infrequently enough that explicit policy handling (exclusion, interpolation, or flagging) is operationally acceptable.
Asynchronous event-driven architecture via Telemetry interface can achieve ≤5 minute aggregation latency without requiring synchronous coupling to Vehicle telemetry streams.
NGHE Solution IM boundary remains stable across sub-level decomposition (no new modules required).
open_risks: Timestamp synchronization protocol selection (NTP vs. GPS vs. PTP) depends on infrastructure availability and cost constraints not yet specified. Risk: protocol mismatch with deployed power distribution or vehicle telemetry systems.
Sensor abstraction layer design may introduce performance overhead (adapter pattern indirection) if not carefully optimized. Risk: aggregation latency exceeds 5-minute target.

Confidence bound statistical model parameterization by sensor type and vehicle metadata requires empirical calibration data. Risk: model parameters unavailable or inaccurate for new sensor types or vehicle classes.

Evaluation window boundary policy (exclusion vs. interpolation) may introduce systematic bias in energy_per_ton estimates if policy is not validated against representative data. Risk: acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) becomes unreliable at window boundaries.

Asynchronous event-driven architecture requires careful handling of out-of-order or delayed telemetry events. Risk: aggregation logic produces incorrect energy_per_ton if event ordering assumptions are violated.

High safety criticality context suggests energy_per_ton may be used for autonomous decision-making (e.g., route optimization, load balancing). Risk: confidence bounds must be validated to ≥95% coverage probability; under-specification of confidence model may lead to unsafe decisions.
evidence_needed: Specification of timestamp synchronization protocol (NTP, GPS, or PTP) with clock skew tolerance justification and infrastructure compatibility assessment.
Sensor abstraction layer design pattern documentation with performance benchmarks (latency, throughput) and support matrix for load cell and displacement sensor types.

Statistical uncertainty model specification with parameterization by sensor type, vehicle metadata, and measurement window duration; empirical validation data showing ≥95% confidence bound coverage probability.

Evaluation window boundary policy specification with rationale (exclusion, interpolation, or flagging) and validation against representative data showing no systematic bias in energy_per_ton estimates.

Event-driven Telemetry interface specification with ordering guarantees, latency bounds, and handling rules for out-of-order or delayed events; end-to-end integration test results demonstrating ≤5 minute aggregation latency.

Acceptance test criteria for all five sub-FRs: timestamp synchronization accuracy ≥95% within ±100ms, confidence bounds coverage ≥95%, sensor calibration drift <2% over evaluation period, energy_per_ton computation latency <5 minutes, boundary condition handling correctness verified over representative evaluation windows.
